curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use gradient rules to identify and construct parallel and perpendicular lines, write equations of lines meeting given conditions, and connect line relationships to design choices in real-world infrastructure and navigation. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How can gradient rules help us design and explain lines that stay aligned or intersect safely in the real world?

knowing_world.focus: Parallel lines share the same gradient, while perpendicular lines have gradients that multiply to -1 (negative reciprocals). Students explore these rules by manipulating lines and writing equations.
key_concepts: Parallel lines have the same gradient
Equation of a line y = mx + c
Effect of changing intercept while keeping gradient constant
tools: Interactive sketch: Explore Parallel Lines (manipulate slope and intercepts, compare lines, write equations)
activities: Change the intercept but keep the gradient the same and describe what changes and what stays the same.
Write an equation for each line shown in the sketch.

human_story.focus: Engineers use parallel and perpendicular line rules in city planning (roads, pavements, railways) so structures stay aligned or intersect safely, affecting access and navigation for people with different needs.
justice_prompts: Why might perpendicular and parallel lines matter more than curves when designing a metro map for access and navigation?
When infrastructure is poorly planned, who is most affected, and how can maths support fairer spaces?

expressive_self.focus: Students use metaphor, sketching, and reflection to express what parallel and perpendicular lines feel like in mood, movement, and everyday life.
activities: Write: “A parallel line is like…” or “Perpendicular lines remind me of…”
Sketch examples of these lines as they appear in your daily world.
Record a short reflection on what these line relationships feel like.
Reflect: Which feels more like you today—consistent and aligned, or shifting and intersecting?

designed_world.focus: Students investigate perpendicular line relationships by dragging points to see gradients change, then apply the rules to design safe intersections and city-grid systems.
tools: Interactive sketch: Explore Perpendicular Lines (drag a point, observe slopes, check m1 × m2)
design_challenges: Design a corner crossing between two paths using perpendicular lines and label the gradients.
Create a system of parallel and perpendicular paths and test whether it could work as a city grid.

regulation_supports: Body map: Stretch arms straight out (parallel), then rotate one arm 90° (perpendicular) and notice how it feels.
Breathe: Inhale as one arm lifts, exhale as the other rotates, noticing the transition between aligned and rotated states.
wellbeing_prompts: How did your energy move today—what moments felt aligned or surprising?

pathways.name: Final Task - Lines That Align
pathways.focus: Create and justify a design using parallel and perpendicular lines, then construct equations for lines parallel and perpendicular to a given line through given points.
products: A design using 2 parallel and 2 perpendicular lines (graph or sketch)
Equations of each line in the design
For y = 3x + 2: equation of a parallel line through (0, -4)
For y = 3x + 2: equation of a perpendicular line through (2, 1)
An explanation (writing, drawing, or voice) of why perpendicular gradients multiply to -1, optionally with a geometric argument
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding: Explains gradient relationships clearly
Fluency & Accuracy: Equations and calculations are correct and clearly shown
Application to Problems: Applies gradient rules in design/modelling tasks
Independence & Reflection: Makes creative or thoughtful choices, optional expressive reflection included

required: Plot coordinates on a Cartesian grid
Understand and use y = mx + c
Work with negative numbers and fractions (for negative reciprocals)

builds_toward: Finding equations of parallel/perpendicular lines through points
Modelling design constraints with linear graphs
